case_name: Tinoco Arbitration (Great Britain v Costa Rica)
court: Sole Arbitrator (William Howard Taft, Chief Justice of the United States)
citation: Tinoco Arbitration (Great Britain v Costa Rica) (1923) 1 RIAA 369
text: TINOCO ARBITRATION (Great Britain v Costa Rica, 1923). ARBITRATOR: William Howard Taft, Chief Justice of the United States. FACTS: In January 1917, Federico Tinoco overthrew the constitutional government of Costa Rica and established a new regime. The Tinoco government was recognized by some states but not by others, notably the United States and Great Britain. During its period of rule (1917-1919), the Tinoco government granted certain oil concessions to a British company and issued banknotes through the Royal Bank of Canada. When the Tinoco regime was overthrown in 1919, the restored constitutional government enacted legislation invalidating all contracts and transactions made by the Tinoco administration, including the oil concessions and the Royal Bank transactions. Great Britain brought a claim on behalf of the British oil company and the Royal Bank of Canada, arguing that the acts of the Tinoco government were binding on Costa Rica under international law. Costa Rica argued that the Tinoco government was not a legitimate government and therefore its acts could not bind the state. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether the Tinoco government was a government in the sense of international law, such that its acts were binding on Costa Rica. (2) Whether non-recognition by certain states (including the United States) affected the legal status of the Tinoco government. (3) Whether successor governments are bound by the obligations undertaken by predecessor de facto governments. HOLDING: Arbitrator Taft held that the Tinoco government was a de facto government of Costa Rica under international law and that its acts were therefore binding on the state. The key criteria for recognition of a government under international law are (a) effective control of the territory and (b) habitual obedience of the population. The Tinoco regime satisfied both criteria during its period of rule. REASONING: (1) EFFECTIVENESS TEST: The Tinoco government maintained effective control over Costa Rican territory for over two years. It exercised all the functions of a sovereign government, maintained order, and was obeyed by the population. Under the objective test of government legitimacy in international law, these facts were determinative. (2) NON-RECOGNITION IS NOT DETERMINATIVE: The fact that certain states, including the United States, did not recognize the Tinoco government did not affect its status as a government under international law. Recognition is a political act and the absence of recognition by individual states does not determine the legal character of a regime that factually exercises governmental authority. Taft stated: 'The non-recognition by other nations of a government claiming to be a national personality, is usually appropriate evidence that it has not attained the independence and control entitling it by international law to be classed as such. But when recognition vel non of a government is by such nations determined by inquiry, not into its de facto sovereignty and complete governmental control, but into its illegitimacy or irregularity of origin, their non-recognition loses something of evidential weight.' (3) OBLIGATIONS BIND SUCCESSOR GOVERNMENTS: When a de facto government exercises effective control, its obligations under international law bind the state and successor governments cannot unilaterally repudiate those obligations. However, the arbitrator ultimately dismissed the British claims on their merits — finding the oil concession was validly granted by the Tinoco government but was surrendered before the government fell, and the Royal Bank transactions were made in bad faith for the personal benefit of the Tinoco family rather than for legitimate governmental purposes. SIGNIFICANCE: The Tinoco Arbitration is a landmark authority in international law on several points: (1) The EFFECTIVENESS PRINCIPLE — a government's legitimacy under international law depends on its effective control of territory and obedience of the population, not on the manner in which it came to power (whether by constitutional means or revolution). (2) The LIMITED ROLE OF RECOGNITION — non-recognition by other states is not conclusive evidence that a regime lacks governmental status under international law. Recognition is a political act that does not determine legal status. (3) CONTINUITY OF STATE OBLIGATIONS — successor governments inherit the international obligations of their predecessor governments, including de facto governments, and cannot unilaterally repudiate them. (4) The case remains the leading authority on the legal status of revolutionary/de facto governments in international law.

citation: Corfu Channel (United Kingdom v Albania) [1949] ICJ Rep 4
text: CORFU CHANNEL CASE (United Kingdom v Albania, 1949). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: On 22 October 1946, two British destroyers struck mines while passing through the Corfu Channel in Albanian territorial waters. Forty-four British sailors were killed and forty-two were injured. The United Kingdom alleged that Albania had laid the mines or knew about them and failed to warn shipping. Subsequently, on 12-13 November 1946, the British Navy conducted a minesweeping operation (Operation Retail) in Albanian territorial waters without Albanian consent, discovering twenty-two moored mines. Albania denied responsibility for the mines and argued that the British minesweeping operation violated Albanian sovereignty. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Albania was responsible under international law for the explosions and the resulting damage. (2) Whether the United Kingdom violated Albanian sovereignty by conducting the minesweeping operation. (3) Whether British warships had a right of innocent passage through the Corfu Channel. HOLDING: (1) The Court held Albania responsible for the mine explosions. Although there was no direct evidence that Albania laid the mines, the Court found that Albania must have known of the minefield and failed to notify shipping or warn the approaching British warships. (2) The Court found that the British minesweeping operation (Operation Retail) violated Albanian sovereignty, constituting a violation of international law regardless of the motive. (3) The Court upheld the right of innocent passage through international straits. REASONING: On Albanian responsibility, the Court applied the doctrine of circumstantial evidence, finding that the mines could not have been laid without Albania's knowledge given its extensive surveillance of the coastline. The Court stated the foundational principle: 'every State's obligation not to allow knowingly its territory to be used for acts contrary to the rights of other States.' On the British minesweeping, the Court rejected the UK's argument that Operation Retail was justified as a form of self-help or intervention. The Court held that the right of intervention cannot be regarded as a legitimate exercise of force, stating: 'The Court can only regard the alleged right of intervention as the manifestation of a policy of force, such as has, in the past, given rise to most serious abuses.' On innocent passage, the Court held that states in time of peace have a right to send their warships through international straits without prior authorization, provided the passage is innocent. SIGNIFICANCE: The Corfu Channel case is a foundational authority in modern international law for several principles: (1) STATE RESPONSIBILITY — a state is responsible for acts committed within its territory that cause harm to other states, even if the state did not directly commit the harmful act; (2) CIRCUMSTANTIAL EVIDENCE — the Court established that circumstantial evidence is admissible in international adjudication; (3) PROHIBITION OF SELF-HELP — unilateral use of force, even to collect evidence or enforce rights, is unlawful; (4) INNOCENT PASSAGE — warships have a right of innocent passage through international straits; (5) The 'no harm' principle — the obligation not to allow territory to be used to harm other states.

case_name: North Sea Continental Shelf Cases (Federal Republic of Germany v Denmark; Federal Republic of Germany v Netherlands)
citation: North Sea Continental Shelf Cases [1969] ICJ Rep 3
text: NORTH SEA CONTINENTAL SHELF CASES (Federal Republic of Germany v Denmark; Federal Republic of Germany v Netherlands, 1969). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: The Federal Republic of Germany (FRG) disputed the application of the equidistance principle for delimiting the continental shelf boundaries with Denmark and the Netherlands in the North Sea. Denmark and the Netherlands argued that Article 6 of the 1958 Geneva Convention on the Continental Shelf, which prescribed the equidistance method, was binding on the FRG as customary international law even though the FRG had not ratified the Convention. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether the equidistance principle in Article 6 of the 1958 Convention had become a rule of customary international law binding on non-parties. (2) What principles and rules of international law are applicable to the delimitation of continental shelf boundaries. HOLDING: The Court held that the equidistance principle had NOT become a rule of customary international law. The delimitation must be effected by agreement in accordance with equitable principles, taking into account all relevant circumstances. REASONING: The Court established the two-element test for the formation of customary international law: (1) EXTENSIVE AND VIRTUALLY UNIFORM STATE PRACTICE — the practice must be both extensive and virtually uniform in the sense of the provision invoked; (2) OPINIO JURIS — state practice must be accompanied by a belief that the practice is required by law (opinio juris sive necessitatis). The Court emphasized: 'Not only must the acts concerned amount to a settled practice, but they must also be such, or be carried out in such a way, as to be evidence of a belief that this practice is rendered obligatory by the existence of a rule of law requiring it.' The Court found insufficient evidence that states applied the equidistance method out of a sense of legal obligation rather than convenience. SIGNIFICANCE: This case is the leading authority on the formation of customary international law, establishing the twin requirements of state practice and opinio juris. It is cited in virtually every subsequent discussion of custom as a source of international law.

case_name: Barcelona Traction, Light and Power Company, Limited (Belgium v Spain)
citation: Barcelona Traction, Light and Power Company, Limited (Belgium v Spain) [1970] ICJ Rep 3
text: BARCELONA TRACTION CASE (Belgium v Spain, 1970). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Barcelona Traction was a Canadian-incorporated company operating in Spain. The majority of its shareholders were Belgian nationals. Spain allegedly caused the company's bankruptcy through various governmental measures. Belgium brought a claim before the ICJ on behalf of its nationals who were shareholders in the company. Spain objected that Belgium lacked standing because the company was incorporated in Canada, not Belgium. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Belgium had standing (jus standi) to exercise diplomatic protection on behalf of shareholders in a company incorporated in a third state (Canada). (2) The Court discussed, in dicta, the concept of obligations erga omnes. HOLDING: The Court found that Belgium lacked standing. Under international law, the right of diplomatic protection of a company belongs to the state of incorporation (Canada), not to the state of nationality of the shareholders (Belgium). REASONING: The Court drew an analogy with municipal law: a company has a separate legal personality from its shareholders. Therefore, when a company is injured, it is the state of incorporation that has the right to exercise diplomatic protection, not the states of nationality of individual shareholders. The Court made its famous obiter dictum on obligations erga omnes: 'An essential distinction should be drawn between the obligations of a State towards the international community as a whole, and those arising vis-à-vis another State in the field of diplomatic protection. By their very nature the former are the concern of all States. In view of the importance of the rights involved, all States can be held to have a legal interest in their protection; they are obligations erga omnes.' The Court listed examples of erga omnes obligations: the outlawing of acts of aggression and genocide, and the protection of basic human rights, including the prohibition of slavery and racial discrimination. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) OBLIGATIONS ERGA OMNES — the Court's dictum established the concept that certain obligations are owed to the international community as a whole, giving all states a legal interest in their observance. This is a cornerstone concept in modern international law. (2) DIPLOMATIC PROTECTION — the case established the rule that diplomatic protection of companies belongs to the state of incorporation. (3) The case influenced the development of the law on state responsibility and standing in international litigation.

citation: The Case of the S.S. Lotus (France v Turkey) [1927] PCIJ Series A, No. 10
text: THE S.S. LOTUS CASE (France v Turkey, 1927). COURT: Permanent Court of International Justice. FACTS: On 2 August 1926, the French mail steamer Lotus collided with the Turkish collier Boz-Kourt on the high seas. The Boz-Kourt sank, and eight Turkish nationals drowned. When the Lotus arrived in Constantinople, Turkey instituted criminal proceedings against Lieutenant Demons, the French officer on watch at the time of the collision. France objected that Turkey lacked jurisdiction to prosecute a French national for acts committed on the high seas. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Turkey violated international law by exercising criminal jurisdiction over a French national for acts committed on the high seas. (2) The scope of state jurisdiction under international law. HOLDING: The PCIJ held that Turkey had not violated international law. In the absence of a specific prohibition in international law, states are free to exercise jurisdiction as they see fit. REASONING: The Court articulated the famous 'Lotus principle': 'International law governs relations between independent States. The rules of law binding upon States therefore emanate from their own free will as expressed in conventions or by usages generally accepted as expressing principles of law... Restrictions upon the independence of States cannot therefore be presumed.' The Court further stated: 'Far from laying down a general prohibition to the effect that States may not extend the application of their laws and the jurisdiction of their courts to persons, property and acts outside their territory, [international law] leaves them in this respect a wide measure of discretion, which is only limited in certain cases by prohibitive rules.' The majority held that because no rule of international law prohibited Turkey from exercising jurisdiction, Turkey was free to do so. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) THE LOTUS PRINCIPLE — the foundational presumption that whatever is not prohibited by international law is permitted. States possess sovereign freedom of action unless a specific rule of international law restricts it. (2) The case established a framework for analyzing state jurisdiction in international law. (3) The Lotus principle has been both influential and controversial — it has been qualified by subsequent developments in international law, particularly the growth of human rights norms and environmental obligations, but remains a frequently cited starting point for jurisdictional analysis.

case_name: Island of Palmas (United States v Netherlands)
citation: Island of Palmas Case (United States v Netherlands) (1928) 2 RIAA 829
text: ISLAND OF PALMAS CASE (United States v Netherlands, 1928). ARBITRATOR: Max Huber. FACTS: The United States claimed sovereignty over the Island of Palmas (Miangas), located between the Philippines and the Dutch East Indies. The US based its claim on the 1898 Treaty of Paris, by which Spain ceded the Philippines (including Palmas) to the United States. The Netherlands argued that it had exercised continuous and peaceful sovereignty over the island since the early 18th century through the Dutch East India Company and subsequent colonial administration. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether the United States acquired sovereignty through the Treaty of Paris (by cession from Spain). (2) Whether Dutch continuous and effective occupation prevailed over a derivative title from discovery/cession. (3) The principles governing the acquisition of territorial sovereignty. HOLDING: Arbitrator Huber awarded sovereignty to the Netherlands, finding that continuous and effective display of state authority (effective occupation) prevails over an inchoate title based on discovery. REASONING: Huber articulated several foundational principles: (1) SOVEREIGNTY AND INDEPENDENCE: 'Sovereignty in the relations between States signifies independence. Independence in regard to a portion of the globe is the right to exercise therein, to the exclusion of any other State, the functions of a State.' (2) CONTINUOUS AND PEACEFUL DISPLAY OF SOVEREIGNTY: The decisive criterion for territorial sovereignty is the 'continuous and peaceful display of territorial sovereignty.' Spain's original discovery could only create an inchoate title, which had to be perfected by effective occupation within a reasonable time. (3) INTER-TEMPORAL LAW: The principle that 'a juridical fact must be appreciated in the light of the law contemporary with it, and not of the law in force at the time when a dispute in regard to it arises.' Rights acquired under the law of the time must be maintained by continuous effective occupation to remain valid under contemporary law. SIGNIFICANCE: The Island of Palmas case is the leading authority on territorial sovereignty and is cited for: (1) The EFFECTIVE OCCUPATION doctrine — discovery alone is insufficient; sovereignty requires continuous and effective exercise of state functions. (2) The INTER-TEMPORAL LAW principle — legal title must be assessed by the law of the time when it was created, but maintained by contemporary standards. (3) The definition of sovereignty as independence and the exclusive right to exercise state functions. The case remains the most cited arbitral decision on territorial acquisition.

case_name: Trail Smelter Arbitration (United States v Canada)
citation: Trail Smelter Arbitration (United States v Canada) (1938/1941) 3 RIAA 1905
text: TRAIL SMELTER ARBITRATION (United States v Canada, 1938/1941). FACTS: A zinc and lead smelter at Trail, British Columbia, Canada, emitted sulphur dioxide fumes that crossed the border and caused damage to crops, trees, and property in the state of Washington, United States. The United States brought a claim against Canada for the transboundary pollution damage. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Canada was responsible under international law for environmental damage caused in the United States by a Canadian industrial facility. (2) The extent of the obligation on states to prevent transboundary harm. HOLDING: The tribunal held that Canada was responsible for the pollution damage. It established the principle that no state has the right to use or permit the use of its territory in such a manner as to cause injury to the territory of another state or the properties or persons therein. REASONING: The tribunal stated: 'Under the principles of international law, as well as of the law of the United States, no State has the right to use or permit the use of its territory in such a manner as to cause injury by fumes in or to the territory of another or the properties or persons therein, when the case is of serious consequence and the injury is established by clear and convincing evidence.' SIGNIFICANCE: (1) SIC UTERE TUO — the Trail Smelter arbitration established the foundational principle of international environmental law that states have a duty not to cause transboundary environmental harm. (2) The case is the cornerstone of international environmental law and Principle 21 of the Stockholm Declaration. (3) It established that state responsibility extends to the acts of private actors within a state's territory when those acts cause damage abroad.

case_name: Reparation for Injuries Suffered in the Service of the United Nations (Advisory Opinion)
citation: Reparation for Injuries Suffered in the Service of the United Nations, Advisory Opinion [1949] ICJ Rep 174
text: REPARATION FOR INJURIES ADVISORY OPINION (1949). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Count Folke Bernadotte, United Nations mediator in Palestine, was assassinated in Jerusalem in September 1948. The UN General Assembly requested an advisory opinion on whether the United Nations, as an international organization, had the legal capacity to bring an international claim against a state for injuries suffered by its agents. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether the United Nations possessed international legal personality. (2) Whether the UN could bring a claim against a responsible state for injuries to its agents. HOLDING: The Court held that the United Nations possesses international legal personality and has the capacity to bring international claims. REASONING: The Court examined the UN Charter and concluded that the Organization was intended to exercise functions and rights that could only be explained on the basis of international personality. The Court stated: 'The subjects of law in any legal system are not necessarily identical in their nature or in the extent of their rights, and their nature depends upon the needs of the community.' The UN is 'an international person' — not a state, but possessing objective international personality. This personality is opposable to all states, not just member states. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) INTERNATIONAL LEGAL PERSONALITY — the advisory opinion established that international organizations can possess international legal personality, distinct from and additional to that of their member states. (2) Expanded the concept of subjects of international law beyond states. (3) Established the concept of OBJECTIVE LEGAL PERSONALITY — the UN's personality is binding on all states. (4) Foundation for the law of international organizations.

citation: Nottebohm Case (Liechtenstein v Guatemala) [1955] ICJ Rep 4
text: NOTTEBOHM CASE (Liechtenstein v Guatemala, 1955). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Friedrich Nottebohm was a German national who had been living and doing business in Guatemala since 1905. In 1939, shortly after the outbreak of World War II, Nottebohm obtained Liechtenstein nationality through a rapid naturalization process (he applied on 9 October 1939 and was naturalized on 13 October 1939). He had no genuine connection with Liechtenstein — he did not reside there before or after naturalization and had no business interests there. Guatemala later seized his property during WWII as enemy alien property (treating him as a German national). Liechtenstein brought a claim against Guatemala exercising diplomatic protection on behalf of Nottebohm. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Liechtenstein's grant of nationality to Nottebohm was effective under international law for the purpose of diplomatic protection. (2) The requirements for nationality to be opposable to other states. HOLDING: The Court held that Liechtenstein's claim was inadmissible because the nationality granted to Nottebohm lacked a GENUINE LINK between Nottebohm and Liechtenstein. REASONING: The Court distinguished between a state's sovereign right to grant nationality under its own domestic law and the effectiveness of that nationality on the international plane. For nationality to be recognized internationally, there must be a genuine connection between the individual and the state — based on factors such as habitual residence, centre of interests, family ties, participation in public life, and attachment. The Court stated: 'Nationality is a legal bond having as its basis a social fact of attachment, a genuine connection of existence, interests and sentiments, together with the existence of reciprocal rights and duties.' SIGNIFICANCE: (1) The GENUINE LINK requirement — nationality must reflect a real social fact of attachment between the individual and the state for it to be effective on the international plane. (2) The distinction between domestic and international effectiveness of nationality. (3) Foundational case for the law of diplomatic protection and nationality.

case_name: Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory (Advisory Opinion)
citation: Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, Advisory Opinion [2004] ICJ Rep 136
text: WALL ADVISORY OPINION (2004). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Israel constructed a wall/barrier in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, largely within the West Bank. The UN General Assembly requested an advisory opinion on the legal consequences of the construction. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether the construction of the wall violated international law. (2) The applicability of international humanitarian law and human rights law in occupied territories. (3) The legal consequences for Israel and other states. HOLDING: The Court found that the construction of the wall in occupied territory was contrary to international law. Israel was obligated to dismantle the wall, return seized land, and make reparation for damage caused. All states were under an obligation not to recognize the illegal situation and not to render aid or assistance in maintaining it. REASONING: The Court held that international humanitarian law (Fourth Geneva Convention) and international human rights law (ICCPR, ICESCR, CRC) applied in the occupied territory. The Court affirmed that the right to self-determination is a right erga omnes. The construction of the wall impeded the exercise of the right to self-determination of the Palestinian people and violated provisions of the Fourth Geneva Convention prohibiting the occupying power from making permanent changes to the occupied territory. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Confirmed the EXTRATERRITORIAL APPLICATION of human rights treaties. (2) Affirmed the right of self-determination as an obligation erga omnes. (3) Established the duty of NON-RECOGNITION of illegal territorial situations. (4) Important precedent on the interaction between international humanitarian law and human rights law.

citation: Gabcikovo-Nagymaros Project (Hungary v Slovakia) [1997] ICJ Rep 7
text: GABCIKOVO-NAGYMAROS PROJECT CASE (Hungary v Slovakia, 1997). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Hungary and Czechoslovakia concluded a 1977 Treaty for the construction of a system of locks on the Danube River. Hungary suspended and later abandoned the works in 1989, citing environmental concerns. Slovakia (successor to Czechoslovakia) proceeded with an alternative unilateral solution (Variant C) which involved diverting the Danube. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Hungary was entitled to suspend and abandon the works. (2) Whether Slovakia was entitled to proceed with Variant C. (3) The application of the doctrine of necessity and fundamental change of circumstances (rebus sic stantibus). (4) Environmental obligations in international law. HOLDING: The Court held that Hungary was not entitled to abandon the 1977 Treaty, but Slovakia was also not entitled to put Variant C into operation unilaterally. Both parties breached their treaty obligations. REASONING: The Court examined several defenses raised by Hungary: (1) ECOLOGICAL NECESSITY (Article 25 ILC Articles): The Court accepted that ecological concerns could constitute a state of necessity but found the threshold was not met — the peril must be imminent and the measures must be the only way to safeguard an essential interest. (2) IMPOSSIBILITY OF PERFORMANCE (Article 61 VCLT): Rejected — the legal regime of the treaty had not disappeared. (3) FUNDAMENTAL CHANGE OF CIRCUMSTANCES (Article 62 VCLT): Rejected — the environmental conditions were not a basis of the parties' consent. The Court emphasized the importance of sustainable development in international law: 'Throughout the ages, mankind has, for economic and other reasons, constantly interfered with nature. In the past, this was often done without consideration of the effects upon the environment. Owing to new scientific insights and to a growing awareness of the risks for mankind… new norms and standards have been developed.' SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Leading case on the doctrine of NECESSITY in international law. (2) First ICJ case to address SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT. (3) Important authority on the law of treaties, particularly impossibility and fundamental change of circumstances. (4) Affirmed that environmental concerns can constitute essential interests of a state.

case_name: Arrest Warrant of 11 April 2000 (Democratic Republic of the Congo v Belgium)
citation: Arrest Warrant of 11 April 2000 (Democratic Republic of the Congo v Belgium) [2002] ICJ Rep 3
text: ARREST WARRANT CASE (DRC v Belgium, 2002). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Belgium issued an international arrest warrant against Mr. Abdulaye Yerodia Ndombasi, the sitting Minister for Foreign Affairs of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, charging him with grave breaches of the Geneva Conventions and crimes against humanity. The DRC argued that the warrant violated the immunity from criminal jurisdiction enjoyed by incumbent foreign ministers under international law. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether an incumbent Minister for Foreign Affairs enjoys immunity from criminal jurisdiction in foreign states. (2) Whether the issuance of an arrest warrant against a sitting minister violates international law. HOLDING: The Court held that the issue and circulation of the arrest warrant violated Belgium's obligation to respect the immunity from criminal jurisdiction and the inviolability enjoyed by the incumbent Minister for Foreign Affairs of the DRC. REASONING: The Court found that under customary international law, incumbent Ministers for Foreign Affairs enjoy full immunity from criminal jurisdiction in other states. This immunity is not a personal privilege but is necessary to ensure the effective performance of their functions on behalf of the state. The Court stated: 'In customary international law, the immunities accorded to Ministers for Foreign Affairs are not granted for their personal benefit, but to ensure the effective performance of their functions on behalf of their respective States.' However, the Court clarified that immunity does not equal impunity: individuals can be prosecuted after leaving office, by international criminal tribunals, or in their own national courts. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) IMMUNITY OF STATE OFFICIALS — the case confirmed that incumbent foreign ministers enjoy full immunity from criminal jurisdiction in foreign states. (2) IMMUNITY vs IMPUNITY — importantly distinguished between immunity and impunity, clarifying four situations where criminal proceedings may still be brought. (3) Key case on the interaction between state immunity and international criminal law.

case_name: Military and Paramilitary Activities in and against Nicaragua (Nicaragua v United States)
citation: Military and Paramilitary Activities in and against Nicaragua (Nicaragua v United States) [1986] ICJ Rep 14
text: NICARAGUA v UNITED STATES (1986). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Nicaragua alleged that the United States was responsible for military and paramilitary activities against it, including supporting the Contras (armed opposition groups), mining Nicaraguan harbors, and conducting direct attacks on Nicaraguan territory. The United States argued that its actions were justified as collective self-defence in support of El Salvador against armed attacks from Nicaragua. The US withdrew from the case, refusing to participate in the merits phase. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) The prohibition on the use of force under Article 2(4) UN Charter and customary international law. (2) The right of self-defence under Article 51 UN Charter. (3) The principle of non-intervention. (4) The relationship between treaty law and customary international law. HOLDING: The Court found that the United States had violated its obligations under customary international law not to use force against another state, not to intervene in its affairs, and not to violate its sovereignty. The US was ordered to make reparation. REASONING: The Court made several landmark findings: (1) USE OF FORCE: The Court defined 'armed attack' (the threshold for self-defence) as the most grave form of the use of force, distinguishing it from lesser forms such as providing weapons or logistical support to rebels. Not every use of force gives rise to a right of self-defence — only an armed attack does. (2) SELF-DEFENCE: The right of collective self-defence requires: (a) an actual armed attack, (b) a declaration by the victim state that it has been attacked, and (c) a request for assistance. The US failed to satisfy these requirements. (3) NON-INTERVENTION: The Court reaffirmed the principle that states are prohibited from intervening in the internal affairs of other states, particularly by supporting armed groups in another state. (4) CUSTOMARY LAW: The Court held that customary international law exists independently of and parallel to treaty law (the UN Charter), and the prohibitions on the use of force and intervention exist in both custom and treaty. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Leading authority on the PROHIBITION OF THE USE OF FORCE and the distinction between armed attack and lesser forms of force. (2) Definitive statement on the requirements for COLLECTIVE SELF-DEFENCE. (3) Landmark on the PRINCIPLE OF NON-INTERVENTION. (4) Established that customary international law and treaty law can coexist as independent sources of obligation.

case_name: Genocide Convention Advisory Opinion (Reservations to the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide)
citation: Reservations to the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide, Advisory Opinion [1951] ICJ Rep 15
text: GENOCIDE CONVENTION ADVISORY OPINION (1951). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: The UN General Assembly requested an advisory opinion on whether a state could make reservations to the Genocide Convention and what the legal effects of such reservations would be. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether a state making a reservation to the Genocide Convention could still be regarded as a party. (2) What effect such a reservation has between the reserving state and objecting states. HOLDING: The Court held that a state may make a reservation to the Genocide Convention, provided the reservation is compatible with the object and purpose of the Convention. A state objecting to the reservation can treat the reserving state as not a party. REASONING: The Court developed the OBJECT AND PURPOSE test for treaty reservations — a reservation is permissible only if it is compatible with the object and purpose of the treaty. The Court emphasized the universal character of the Genocide Convention and the fundamental rights it protects. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the OBJECT AND PURPOSE test for treaty reservations, later codified in Article 19 of the 1969 Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties. (2) Recognized the special character of human rights treaties. This advisory opinion fundamentally changed the law of treaty reservations.

case_name: Application of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide (Bosnia and Herzegovina v Serbia and Montenegro)
citation: Application of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide (Bosnia and Herzegovina v Serbia and Montenegro) [2007] ICJ Rep 43
text: GENOCIDE CASE (Bosnia v Serbia, 2007). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Bosnia and Herzegovina alleged that Serbia was responsible for genocide committed during the 1992-1995 Bosnian War, particularly the Srebrenica massacre of July 1995 in which over 7,000 Bosnian Muslim men and boys were killed. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Serbia committed genocide or was complicit in genocide. (2) Whether Serbia violated the obligation to prevent and punish genocide. (3) The standard of proof for genocide. (4) Attribution of acts of non-state actors to a state. HOLDING: The Court found that Serbia had not committed genocide, nor was it complicit in genocide. However, Serbia had violated its obligation to prevent genocide (specifically the Srebrenica genocide) and its obligation to cooperate with the ICTY. REASONING: The Court adopted a high standard of proof: genocide requires proof that the perpetrator intended to destroy, in whole or in part, a national, ethnic, racial, or religious group as such (dolus specialis). For attribution, the Court applied the 'effective control' test from Nicaragua — acts of the Republika Srpska forces could not be attributed to Serbia unless Serbia exercised effective control over those forces in the specific operations at issue. Serbia's failure to prevent genocide was established because Serbia had the means to influence the Republika Srpska forces and knew of the serious risk of genocide. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) First time the ICJ ruled on state responsibility for genocide. (2) Confirmed the EFFECTIVE CONTROL test as the standard for attribution. (3) Established that the obligation to prevent genocide is an obligation of conduct, not result. (4) Set a high evidentiary standard for genocide claims.

case_name: Tehran Hostages Case (United States Diplomatic and Consular Staff in Tehran)
citation: United States Diplomatic and Consular Staff in Tehran (United States of America v Iran) [1980] ICJ Rep 3
text: TEHRAN HOSTAGES CASE (USA v Iran, 1980). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: On 4 November 1979, Iranian militants seized the United States Embassy in Tehran and took its diplomatic and consular staff hostage. Iran failed to take any action to prevent the seizure or to secure the release of the hostages. The situation lasted for 444 days. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Iran was responsible under international law for the seizure and continued detention of US diplomatic personnel. (2) The obligations of a host state regarding the protection of foreign diplomatic missions. HOLDING: The Court unanimously found Iran responsible for violations of the Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations (1961), the Vienna Convention on Consular Relations (1963), and customary international law. REASONING: The Court distinguished two phases: In the initial phase, the militants' actions could not be attributed to Iran because they were not acting as agents of the state. However, Iran failed in its obligation to protect the embassy. In the second phase, when the Ayatollah Khomeini and other state organs endorsed the occupation and the detention, the acts of the militants became acts of the Iranian state through adoption. The Court emphasized: 'The approval given [by the Ayatollah] and his decision to perpetuate them, translated continuing occupation of the Embassy and detention of the hostages into acts of that State.' SIGNIFICANCE: (1) The ADOPTION principle — a state becomes responsible for acts of private individuals when it subsequently endorses or adopts those acts as its own. (2) The inviolability of diplomatic premises and the duty of the host state to protect them. (3) Key precedent on diplomatic law and state responsibility.

case_name: Nuclear Tests Cases (Australia v France; New Zealand v France)
text: NUCLEAR TESTS CASES (1974). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Australia and New Zealand challenged France's atmospheric nuclear testing in the Pacific. During the proceedings, France announced that it would cease atmospheric testing. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether France was bound by its unilateral declaration to cease atmospheric testing. (2) Whether the cases had become moot. HOLDING: The Court held that France's unilateral declarations created a legal obligation and that the cases were therefore moot. REASONING: The Court established that unilateral declarations by states can create legally binding obligations when made publicly and with the intention of being bound: 'It is well recognized that declarations made by way of unilateral acts, concerning legal or factual situations, may have the effect of creating legal obligations... When it is the intention of the State making the declaration that it should become bound according to its terms, that intention confers on the declaration the character of a legal undertaking.' The binding character of such a declaration is based on good faith. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) UNILATERAL DECLARATIONS — established that states can create binding legal obligations through unilateral public declarations, based on the principle of good faith. (2) Key precedent on the legal effects of state declarations. (3) Important example of mootness in international adjudication.

case_name: Anglo-Norwegian Fisheries Case (United Kingdom v Norway)
citation: Fisheries Case (United Kingdom v Norway) [1951] ICJ Rep 116
text: ANGLO-NORWEGIAN FISHERIES CASE (UK v Norway, 1951). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: The United Kingdom challenged Norway's method of drawing straight baselines along its deeply indented coastline to delimit its territorial sea. The UK argued that baselines must follow the low-water mark along the coast. Norway argued that its method of straight baselines connecting the outermost points of its skjærgaard (rock rampart of islands and reefs) was consistent with international law and had been applied without objection for decades. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Norway's system of straight baselines was consistent with international law. (2) The criteria for drawing baselines along deeply indented coastlines. HOLDING: The Court upheld Norway's system of straight baselines. REASONING: The Court found that the method of straight baselines was not contrary to international law, particularly for states with deeply indented coastlines or fringes of islands. The Court identified several criteria: the baselines must not depart from the general direction of the coast, the sea areas within the baselines must be sufficiently closely linked to the land domain, and certain economic interests peculiar to the region may be taken into account. The Court also noted that Norway's practice had been applied consistently for decades without objection from other states. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) The case directly influenced Article 4 of the 1958 Geneva Convention on the Territorial Sea and Article 7 of UNCLOS (1982) on STRAIGHT BASELINES. (2) Foundational case for the law of the sea on baseline delimitation. (3) Established that geographic realities determine permissible methods of maritime delimitation.

case_name: South West Africa Cases (Ethiopia v South Africa; Liberia v South Africa)
citation: South West Africa Cases (Ethiopia v South Africa; Liberia v South Africa), Second Phase [1966] ICJ Rep 6
text: SOUTH WEST AFRICA CASES (1966). COURT: International Court of Justice. FACTS: Ethiopia and Liberia brought proceedings against South Africa regarding its administration of South West Africa (Namibia) under the League of Nations mandate. They alleged that South Africa had violated its obligations under the mandate by introducing and enforcing apartheid in the territory. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Ethiopia and Liberia had legal standing to bring the claims. (2) The distinction between individual state interests and community interests in international law. HOLDING: The Court, by the President's casting vote (7-7), found that Ethiopia and Liberia lacked standing because they had no individual legal right or interest in the subject matter of their claims. REASONING: The majority held that while the League of Nations mandate imposed obligations on South Africa, these obligations were owed to the League and its organs, not to individual member states. Ethiopia and Liberia had not shown that they had any individual legal right or interest in the performance of the mandate. The case was highly controversial, and the decision was widely criticized. It directly led to the Court's distinction in Barcelona Traction (1970) between obligations owed to particular states and obligations erga omnes owed to the international community as a whole. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) The case catalyzed the development of the concept of OBLIGATIONS ERGA OMNES and COMMUNITY INTERESTS in international law. (2) The controversial decision led to significant reforms in the ICJ's composition and approach. (3) Key case on standing (locus standi) in international law.
